title: Efficient Language-Guided Radiance Field Reconstruction for 3D Language Field Modeling
problem: Current methods for radiance field reconstruction struggle to incorporate open-vocabulary language inputs efficiently, especially in complex scenes with high visual fidelity In particular, scalable 3D language field modeling remains difficult when queries must stay open-vocabulary and mask-free.
existing_methods: Methods like Gaussian Splatting [2022] and Mip-NeRF360 [Barron et al.] provide high-quality radiance field rendering but lack efficient integration with language inputs. Lerf [CLIP embeddings] supports open-vocabulary queries but requires region proposals or masks, limiting scalability.
motivation: The ability to reconstruct high-fidelity 3D scenes from natural language descriptions is critical for applications such as virtual reality and augmented reality, where semantic understanding of the scene is essential This matters because 3D language field modeling should support efficient open-vocabulary interaction in real scenes.
hypothesis: Integrating CLIP embeddings with Gaussian Splatting can enable efficient and high-quality radiance field reconstruction guided by natural language descriptions without requiring explicit region proposals or masks within a 3D language field.
method: We propose a method that combines CLIP embeddings with Gaussian Splatting to guide the reconstruction of radiance fields. The CLIP embeddings are used to encode the language input, which is then integrated into the Gaussian Splatting framework to refine the spatial distribution of points and their associated radiance values. This approach allows for direct language-guided scene generation without the need for additional segmentation or masking steps The resulting representation is optimized specifically as a 3D language field rather than a generic text-guided scene generator.
evaluation: Evaluate the method on benchmark datasets using quantitative metrics such as PSNR and SSIM, alongside qualitative assessments of language-guided scene generation. Compare against existing methods like Lerf and Gaussian Splatting to demonstrate improvements in both efficiency and visual quality.
significance: This work bridges the gap between language understanding and 3D scene reconstruction, enabling more intuitive and semantically rich interactions with 3D environments This would strengthen efficient 3D language field modeling for open-vocabulary interaction.
caveats: The method assumes access to pre-trained CLIP models and may require fine-tuning for specific domains. Additionally, the performance may degrade in scenes with highly ambiguous or complex language descriptions.
